title_en: The Tabi'een: The Generation That Preserved the Sunnah
content_en:
The generation of the Tabi'een — the Successors, or more precisely the Followers, meaning those who followed in the footsteps of the Companions — occupies a central place in the Siyar A'lam al-Nubala. These were the men and women who had not themselves seen the Prophet but who had sat at the feet of those who had, receiving from them the living transmission of the prophetic teaching and example. The Tabi'een generation spans roughly the period from the mid-7th century CE through to the early 8th century, and it encompasses some of the most important figures in the history of Islamic learning.

The scholarly tradition distinguished within the Tabi'een between the Kibar (senior Tabi'een, who had extensive contact with many Companions) and the Sighar (junior Tabi'een, who had contact with only a few or younger Companions). This distinction mattered for the evaluation of hadith: a Senior Tabi'i who had sat with Abu Bakr, Umar, and Aisha occupied a different position in the chain of transmission from a Junior Tabi'i who had met only one or two of the younger Companions in their old age. Adh-Dhahabi navigates these distinctions in his biographical entries, noting carefully which Companions each Tabi'i had studied under and how extensive that contact was.

Among the Senior Tabi'een, several figures receive extended treatment in the Siyar that repays close reading. Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib of Medina is recognized by adh-Dhahabi and the broader scholarly tradition as arguably the greatest of the Tabi'een in terms of combined mastery of hadith and fiqh. Born in the caliphate of Umar, he studied under multiple major Companions including Abu Hurairah (whose daughter he married), Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas, and Ibn Abbas. His legal opinions were authoritative in Medina, and subsequent generations of scholars treated his judgments as among the most reliable evidence for the practice of the people of Medina — a crucial source for the Maliki school of law. His entry in the Siyar is one of the longest and most detailed for any Tabi'i, and adh-Dhahabi's assessment of him as 'the master of the Successors in his time in hadith, legal knowledge, and scrupulousness' reflects a consensus that had been building in the tradition for centuries.

Al-Hasan al-Basri — the great preacher, ascetic, and scholar of Basra — represents a different dimension of the Tabi'een generation. His influence was primarily through oral teaching and preaching rather than through a formally organized transmission of hadith, and adh-Dhahabi's treatment of him reflects the complexity of his legacy. Al-Hasan's sermons on the themes of death, accountability, the nature of the Hereafter, and the dangers of worldly attachment shaped the ascetic tradition in early Islam in ways that continued to reverberate for centuries. His relationship to the political conflicts of his time — the fitna that followed the Prophet's death — was one of deliberate withdrawal from partisan involvement, a stance that was both praised as wise caution and criticized by some as a failure of civic responsibility. Adh-Dhahabi records these dimensions without forcing a simple verdict.

Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz — the Umayyad caliph known as the fifth Rightly Guided Caliph in some Sunni traditions — receives one of the most admiring entries in the Siyar among the Tabi'een generation, though his primary significance is as a ruler rather than a scholar. His caliphate (717-720 CE) was marked by an attempt to return the governance of the Muslim state to the standards of the first generation — reduction of the extravagance that had accumulated, restoration of lands improperly seized, equalization of stipends across ethnic and tribal lines. Adh-Dhahabi's portrait of him combines political history with spiritual biography, reflecting the Siyar's consistent concern with the connection between character and achievement.

The female figures among the Tabi'een are represented in the Siyar more briefly than the men but with consistent recognition of their scholarly significance. Hafsa bint Sirin, the sister of the famous scholar Muhammad ibn Sirin of Basra, was known as a great reciter of the Quran who had memorized it to a high level of precision and who taught it to others. Amra bint Abd al-Rahman was among Aisha's most important students, transmitting a large body of hadith from the Mother of the Believers and serving as an authoritative reference for the hadith of Aisha in the generation after her death. These entries, though shorter than those for major male scholars, collectively document the continuation of women's participation in the transmission of Islamic knowledge in the generation after the Companions.

The Tabi'een generation also produced the first systematic commentaries on the Quran, the first organized collections of legal opinions, and the foundational texts of what would become the classical schools of law and theology. The Siyar's coverage of this generation provides essential biographical grounding for understanding how these developments occurred — who was teaching whom, in which cities, under what conditions, and with what intellectual orientations. Understanding the Tabi'een as adh-Dhahabi presents them is therefore understanding the first great consolidation of Islamic scholarly culture, the period in which the living transmission of the prophetic generation was converted into the textual and institutional forms that could carry it forward indefinitely.
